era_context: The early 16th century continued with the Portuguese maritime expansion accelerating under Manuel I, the Ottoman Empire under Bayezid II in its final years, the Ming Dynasty in China under the Zhengde Emperor, and the Mughal Empire emerging as a major power in India under Babur. The Italian Renaissance was flourishing with figures like Michelangelo and Raphael at their creative peaks. In the Americas, the Aztec and Inca empires remained at their territorial zenith, unaware of European contact.

events.title: Portuguese capture of Goa from Bijapur Sultanate
events.description: Afonso de Albuquerque led a Portuguese naval expedition to capture the strategic port city of Goa from the Bijapur Sultanate in November 1510. The conquest involved naval bombardment and assault on the city's fortifications. Goa's capture was significant because it provided the Portuguese with a major base on the Indian coast, facilitating control of Indian Ocean trade routes and serving as a springboard for further expansion. However, the Portuguese would lose the city in 1511 before recapturing it definitively later that year.
events.certainty_note: The capture of Goa in November 1510 is well-documented in Portuguese sources. The date is firmly established.

events.title: Battle of Diu (Portuguese naval victory)
events.description: Portuguese naval forces under Afonso de Albuquerque defeated a combined fleet of the Mamluk Sultanate (Egypt) and the Bijapur Sultanate in the waters off Diu. This naval victory was crucial in establishing Portuguese dominance in the Indian Ocean and preventing Arab and Egyptian interference with Portuguese expansion. The battle demonstrated the superiority of Portuguese naval technology and tactics, particularly the use of cannon-armed ships. The victory secured Portuguese control over key trade routes and discouraged organized resistance to Portuguese expansion in the region.
events.certainty_note: The battle is well-documented in Portuguese sources. The date of February 1510 is firmly established, though some sources place it in late 1509.

events.title: Bayezid II's reign continues in Ottoman Empire
events.description: Bayezid II continued his reign as Ottoman Sultan, though his power was beginning to wane due to age and the rising influence of his son Selim. Bayezid II was known for his administrative reforms, cultural patronage, and relative tolerance toward religious minorities, including Sephardic Jews fleeing Spain. However, by 1510, his authority was being challenged by his sons, and the empire was experiencing internal instability. His reign would end in 1512 with his abdication in favor of Selim I.
events.certainty_note: Bayezid II's reign dates and the succession crisis are well-documented in Ottoman administrative records.

events.title: Zhengde Emperor's reign in Ming China
events.description: The Zhengde Emperor continued his reign, characterized by increasing eunuch influence and the emperor's personal indulgence. The eunuch Liu Jin wielded enormous power over state affairs, leading to corruption and institutional decay. Despite these governance issues, the Ming Dynasty maintained economic prosperity and cultural vitality. The period saw continued artistic and literary achievements, though political stability was compromised by the emperor's disinterest in traditional governance.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Zhengde Emperor's reign and eunuch influence are well-documented in Ming records.

events.title: Babur's campaigns in Central Asia and India
events.description: Babur, the future founder of the Mughal Empire, was engaged in military campaigns to expand his territory in Central Asia and northern India. He was consolidating power and eliminating rival sultanates. Babur's military innovations, including the use of gunpowder artillery and cavalry tactics, were beginning to reshape warfare in the region. His campaigns in this period laid the groundwork for his eventual conquest of northern India and the establishment of the Mughal Empire.
events.certainty_note: Babur's campaigns in this period are documented in his autobiography, though specific events in 1510 are less precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Diu is sometimes dated to February 1510 or late 1509; sources vary slightly on the exact date. The significance of the battle in preventing Arab/Mamluk expansion is sometimes overstated—the Mamluks were already in decline and did not mount sustained resistance to Portuguese expansion. The capture of Goa in 1510 was temporary; the Portuguese loss of the city in 1511 is sometimes downplayed in triumphalist Portuguese narratives.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1510 is rich, particularly for Portuguese, Ottoman, and Chinese sources. Portuguese colonial records are detailed and abundant. However, sources from the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia are less abundant in European archives. The perspective is heavily weighted toward European and Ottoman court records. Indigenous perspectives from conquered regions are largely absent. Modern scholarship has questioned the 'triumphalist' narrative of Portuguese conquest, noting the resilience of local trading networks.

graph_edges.note: Both events represent Portuguese expansion in India under Albuquerque in the same year
Portuguese naval victory at Diu enabled the conquest of Malacca in 1511
